title: Angles and triangles
source: Essential Mathematics for the Australian Curriculum, Year 9 (Cambridge, Greenwood et al. 2024) — Chapter 7 Geometry, Section 7A. Images are direct crops of the original textbook pages.

workedExamples.title: Example 1 — Finding supplementary and complementary angles
Example 2 — Finding unknown angles in triangles
Example 3 — Finding exterior and other angles

questions.topic: angle types & rules (fill in the blank)
questions.aiNotes: a=right, b=180 (straight), c=revolution, d=obtuse, e=acute, f=180, g=90, h=supplementary, i=180, j=equal.

questions.topic: naming triangle types from a description
questions.aiNotes: a=isosceles, b=obtuse(-angled), c=equilateral, d=isosceles, e=acute(-angled), f=scalene, g=right-angled.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: mixing up the side-length family (scalene/isosceles/equilateral) with the angle family (acute/right/obtuse)
misconceptions.reason: triangles are classified two different ways and a student may answer with the wrong family

questions.topic: naming and estimating angle types from a diagram
questions.aiNotes: This is a hands-on estimate-then-measure-with-a-protractor task (name the angle type shown, estimate its size, then verify with a real protractor). There is no single verifiable numeric answer without the official answer key or a physical protractor — focus the conversation on how to recognise each angle TYPE (acute/right/obtuse/reflex/revolution) from the arc drawn in the diagram, not on pinning down an exact degree value.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: assuming any two angles shown together must be S or C without adding them first
misconceptions.reason: e.g. 100°,90° sum to 190°, which is neither

questions.aiNotes: a) 55: supp 125, comp 35. b) 74: supp 106, comp 16. c) 89: supp 91, comp 1. d) 22: supp 158, comp 68. e) 38: supp 142, comp 52. f) 47: supp 133, comp 43.

misconceptions.reason: using 90-x for supplementary or 180-x for complementary

questions.aiNotes: Each part shows rays at a point where the marked angles form a right angle, a straight line, or a full revolution together with a°. Without the official answer key, do not assert exact values — instead help the student identify which relationship applies (do the rays look like they form a right angle / straight line / full circle?) and set up their own equation.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: assuming every diagram is 'angles on a straight line' (180°)
misconceptions.reason: some of these are actually a full revolution (360°) or a marked right angle (90°) — the student needs to read which one applies from the diagram itself

questions.aiNotes: a) 100+40+a=180 -> a=40, isosceles. b) 80+70+b=180 -> b=30, acute scalene. c) 40+c+50=180 -> c=90, right-angled scalene. d) only one angle (d°) is given with tick marks and no other numeric angle — not solvable without the official answer/more info. e) isosceles (tick marks), base 40° given twice -> e=100. f) right angle mark + 45° -> f=45 (45-45-90 triangle). g) 30+g+50=180 -> g=100, obtuse scalene. h) 60+h+60=180 -> h=60, equilateral. i) 35+75+i=180 -> i=70, acute scalene.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: subtracting only one of the two given angles from 180
misconceptions.reason: e.g. thinking a=140 instead of a=40 in part a

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: naming the triangle type without checking whether two computed angles are equal
misconceptions.reason: e.g. missing that part a is isosceles because 40°=40°

questions.topic: triangle angle sum with exterior/isosceles diagrams
questions.aiNotes: d) 90+30+a=180 -> a=60, and x is the exterior angle supplementary to a, so x=120 (right-angle mark visible). Other parts (s, t, r, b, c, d) involve tick marks and angles positioned outside the triangle (exterior angles) that are genuinely easy to misread from a compressed image — do not assert values for those without the official answer key; focus on discussing which rule (angle sum, isosceles base angles, exterior angle) looks relevant.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: treating an angle drawn outside the triangle as if it were an interior angle
misconceptions.reason: several of these diagrams mark the EXTERIOR angle, which is supplementary to the interior angle at that vertex, not equal to it

questions.aiNotes: Minute hand moves 6°/minute (360°/60min). a) 1 hour=360°. b) 1/4 hour=90°. c) 10 min=60°. d) 15 min=90°. e) 72 min=432° (more than one revolution). f) 1 min=6°. g) 2 hours=720°. h) 1 day=8640°.

misconceptions.reason: confuses the minute hand's rate with something like the second hand or a mis-scaled rate
doesn't realise the hand can go past a full revolution

questions.topic: exterior angle and vertically opposite angles
questions.aiNotes: Both parts combine an isosceles triangle with vertically opposite / exterior angle relationships (similar style to Example 3b). Do not assert exact values without the official answer key — discuss which angles are equal (vertically opposite, or the two base angles of the isosceles triangle) and which relationship (angle sum vs exterior angle theorem) fits.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: treating the hour hand as fixed exactly on its number
misconceptions.reason: forgets the hour hand moves 0.5°/minute between numbers, e.g. at 6:30 it's halfway between 6 and 7

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: giving the reflex angle instead of the smaller angle
misconceptions.reason: the question asks for the acute/obtuse angle between the hands, which should not exceed 180°

questions.aiNotes: Involves a tangent (90° to the radius) and a given 120° angle; asks the student to explain why x=330. This needs careful reading of the specific circle diagram — do not assert the derivation without the official answer key, but you can prompt the student to start from 'the tangent is 90° to the radius' and think about what's left over around the centre point.

O is the centre, so OA and OB are both radii (equal), making triangle OAB isosceles with apex angle 116° at O. The two base angles (including angle OAB) are therefore equal: (180-116)/2 = 32. This one is derivable from a clean geometric fact (radii are equal) — reasonably confident, but still flag it as AI-derived, not the official wording, until confirmed.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: not recognising OA and OB as radii
misconceptions.reason: misses that both are radii of the same circle and therefore equal, which is what makes the triangle isosceles

questions.aiNotes: a) a=85,b=75 -> angle XYW (interior, at Y) = 180-85-75=20, so exterior angle XYZ = 180-20 = 160. b) a=105,b=60 -> interior at Y = 180-105-60=15, exterior XYZ=165. c) General proof: interior angle at Y = 180-a-b, and XYZ is supplementary to it (straight line XYW), so XYZ = 180-(180-a-b) = a+b. Note: the question explicitly says not to just quote the exterior angle theorem — walk through the straight-line/angle-sum steps.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: just applying the exterior angle theorem directly (XYZ=a+b) without the intermediate steps
misconceptions.reason: the question explicitly asks NOT to use the theorem as a shortcut, but to derive it from angle sum + straight line facts

questions.aiNotes: Let interior angles be a,b,c with a+b+c=180. Each exterior angle = 180 minus its adjacent interior angle, so the three exterior angles sum to 540-(a+b+c) = 540-180 = 360.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: stating '360° because it's a revolution' with no derivation
misconceptions.reason: asserts the result without connecting it back to the 180° interior angle sum, which is what the question asks to prove

questions.topic: algebra with angles at a point / in a triangle
questions.aiNotes: a) right-angle mark shown split into 3x and x: 3x+x=90 -> x=22.5. c) four angles at a point (revolution): 4x+2x+3x+x=360 -> x=36. d) isosceles triangle, apex x°, two equal base angles (x+15)° each: x+2(x+15)=180 -> 3x+30=180 -> x=50. Parts b, e, f involve reading exactly which rays bound which angle in a compact diagram — genuinely easy to misread, so do not assert values for those without the official answer key.

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: using 180 instead of 360 for a full-revolution diagram (part c)
misconceptions.reason: forgets these angles go all the way around a point rather than along a straight line
